supporting_evidence: History of 3 UTIs in 18 months with most recent treatment 6 days ago
E. coli with quinolone resistance — possible treatment failure with TMP-SMX
Immunosuppressed patients may not present with classic UTI symptoms (dysuria, frequency)
Generalized weakness and malaise can be the presenting complaint of UTI in elderly
Weakness and dizziness are common symptoms of dehydration in elderly
Recent illness and antibiotic course may have reduced oral intake
BP 118/68 is relatively low for this patient's age group
IV fluid challenge may be both diagnostic and therapeutic
Methotrexate is known to cause fatigue, weakness, and bone marrow suppression
Concurrent TMP-SMX may potentiate methotrexate toxicity via folate pathway interaction
Renal clearance of methotrexate may be impaired in elderly diabetic patient
CBC should be checked to assess for cytopenias
Dual immunosuppression (methotrexate + prednisone) impairs immune surveillance
Immunosuppressed patients can harbour serious infections with minimal symptoms
Prednisone may mask inflammatory markers and fever
Broad infectious workup warranted given immunocompromised status
Near-fall getting out of bed suggests positional component
Beta-blocker therapy impairs heart rate compensation for postural change
Diabetic autonomic neuropathy may further impair baroreceptor response
Formal lying-standing blood pressure assessment indicated

reasoning: The differential reflects a mix of common and concerning diagnoses. UTI recurrence leads given the recent treatment history and pattern of recurrent infections. Dehydration and medication toxicity are common contributors to weakness in elderly polypharmacy patients. The immunosuppressed status is noted, and occult infection is flagged as a don't-miss diagnosis — however, the normal vital signs and absence of overt infection signs suggest this is less likely than the more common explanations. Further investigation with CBC, CMP, urinalysis, and cultures will help narrow the differential.
